answer:
The American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association (ACC/AHA) recommends oral anticoagulation with direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) as the preferred treatment for patients with atrial fibrillation and a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 2 or more in men or 3 or more in women. This is based on the ACC/AHA/HRS Atrial Fibration Guideline, which states that DOACs are preferred over warfarin in most eligible patients [1].

Therefore, for most people needing blood thinners for AFib, a DOAC would be a better option than warfarin.

References:
[1] Anticoagulation in Atrial Fibrillation, ACC/AHA/HRS, 2024.

arguments.query: DOAC vs warfarin for atrial fibrillation treatment in adults

result.query: DOAC vs warfarin for atrial fibrillation treatment in adults

results.content: Oral anticoagulation is recommended for patients with atrial fibrillation and a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 2 or more in men or 3 or more in women. Direct oral anticoagulants are preferred over warfarin in most eligible patients.

For adults and adolescents with asthma, as-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol is preferred as reliever therapy over short-acting beta-agonist alone, even at the mildest step of treatment, to reduce exacerbation risk.
results.citation: Global Strategy for Asthma Management, GINA, 2024

results.content: For outpatient community-acquired pneumonia in healthy adults, amoxicillin or doxycycline is recommended. For inpatients, beta-lactam plus macrolide combination therapy is standard.
An A1C goal for many nonpregnant adults with diabetes of <7% without significant hypoglycemia is appropriate. Metformin is the preferred initial pharmacologic agent for type 2 diabetes.
results.source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
results.citation: Glycemic Targets & Pharmacologic Approaches, American Diabetes Association, 2024

results.content: For moderate to severe depression, a combination of antidepressant medication and high-intensity psychological therapy such as cognitive behavioral therapy is recommended over either treatment alone.
results.source: NICE Guideline on Depression in Adults
results.citation: Management of Depression in Adults, NICE, 2022

results.content: A blood pressure target of <130/80 mmHg is recommended for most adults with diabetes and hypertension. ACE inhibitors or ARBs are preferred first-line agents, particularly in the presence of albuminuria.
results.source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
results.citation: Hypertension Management in Diabetes, American Diabetes Association, 2024

results.content: A blood pressure target of <130/80 mmHg is recommended for most adults with hypertension. First-line agents include thiazide diuretics, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and calcium channel blockers.

High-intensity statin therapy is recommended for patients with clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and for primary prevention in adults 40-75 years with LDL-C >=190 mg/dL or diabetes with additional risk factors.

Aspirin for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease is not routinely recommended due to bleeding risk, except in select high-risk adults 40-70 years after a risk-benefit discussion with their clinician.
results.source: ACC/AHA Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease Guideline
results.citation: Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease, ACC/AHA, 2019

results.content: The USPSTF recommends screening for colorectal cancer in all adults aged 45 to 75 years. Screening options include colonoscopy every 10 years, annual FIT testing, or stool DNA-FIT every 1-3 years.

result.disclaimer: Evidence excerpts for professional review; not a diagnosis.
